title: Is Taste Enough?
description: A short guided reasoning flow about taste, harm, necessity, and consistency.

flow.title: Is Taste Enough?
flow.description: A short guided reasoning flow about taste, harm, necessity, and consistency.

taste.title: Is taste enough?
taste.text: Is it okay to harm an animal mainly because we enjoy the taste?

choices.label: No, taste alone is not enough

necessity.title: Preference or necessity?
necessity.text: If taste alone is not enough, what changes when eating animals is no longer necessary for health or survival?

dog-test.title: The dog test
dog-test.text: If someone enjoyed the taste of dog meat, would that pleasure be enough to justify killing a healthy dog?

choices.label: Yes, if they really enjoy it

animal-difference.title: Which difference matters?
animal-difference.text: If dogs and pigs are treated differently, what morally relevant trait explains the difference?

alternatives.title: Available alternatives
alternatives.text: When plant-based meals can meet the same need, the choice becomes less about survival and more about preference.

choices.label: So consistency becomes the key question
That changes the weight of taste

survival.title: Edge cases
survival.text: Survival cases may justify things that ordinary preference does not. Most daily food choices are not survival emergencies.

consistency.title: Consistency check
consistency.text: If two animals can suffer similarly, should similar interests receive similar moral consideration?

pleasure-limit.title: Limits of pleasure
pleasure-limit.text: Most ethics place limits on pleasure when another being is seriously harmed. Where would you draw that line?

choices.label: I would allow most preference-based harm

intelligence.title: Intelligence as a test
intelligence.text: If intelligence decides moral worth, do less intelligent humans deserve less protection from harm?

culture.title: Culture explains, but does it justify?
culture.text: Culture can explain why a practice feels normal. Does normality itself make the practice ethical?

choices.label: No, normal is not the same as right

sentience.title: Sentience
sentience.text: If the central issue is the ability to suffer, then pigs, cows, chickens, dogs, and many other animals belong inside the moral circle.

category.title: Categories and circles
category.text: Categories can guide affection and responsibility, but they can also hide inconsistent treatment of similar suffering.

taste-weight.title: What is taste worth?
taste-weight.text: Taste has value. The question is whether a brief preference outweighs an animal's entire experience of confinement, fear, and death.

choices.label: That points toward avoiding unnecessary harm
I would want to write my own threshold

gentle-conclusion.title: A practical conclusion
gentle-conclusion.text: If unnecessary harm is avoidable, reducing or replacing animal products becomes a consistent way to act on that reasoning.

hard-conclusion.title: A demanding position
hard-conclusion.text: Allowing preference-based harm is consistent, but it asks us to accept many harms we usually reject in other contexts.

open-question.title: Open question
open-question.text: If category membership matters most, the next step is explaining why that category should override comparable suffering.

future-input.title: Input node placeholder
future-input.text: Future versions will let viewers write a personal threshold here, then route the flow based on their answer.

next-steps.title: What would you like to do next?
next-steps.text: If this resonated with you, here are a few low-pressure next steps to keep exploring.
